title: Past Tense Conjugation
explanation: Lebanese past tense is formed by adding suffixes to the verb root. Unlike MSA, Lebanese simplifies some forms — notably, masculine and feminine 'you (singular)' share the same base with only the feminine adding ـي. Here are two essential verbs fully conjugated.

examples.english: I went to the market
We ate together
They saw him yesterday
She arrived home

title: Conditional with إذا (iza)
explanation: إذا (iza) means 'if' and introduces real, possible conditions. It is typically followed by a past-tense verb (even when the meaning is present/future). The result clause uses رح or the b- present tense.

examples.english: If you want to come, let me know
examples.breakdown: إذا (if) + بدك (you want) + تيجي (to come) — خبرني (tell me)

examples.english: If there's time, I'll visit you
examples.breakdown: إذا (if) + في (there is) + وقت (time) — بزورك (I visit you)

examples.arabic: إذا ما حبيت الأكل، ما تاكل
examples.transliteration: iza mā ḥabbēt il-akl, mā tēkol
examples.english: If you don't like the food, don't eat
examples.breakdown: إذا (if) + ما حبيت (you didn't like) — ما تاكل (don't eat)

examples.english: If he comes early, we'll go together
examples.breakdown: إذا (if) + اجا (he came) + بكير (early) — منروح (we go) + سوا (together)

title: The Wish Pattern — يا ريت (ya rēt)
explanation: يا ريت (ya rēt) expresses a wish or longing — 'I wish / if only.' It is followed by a verb (past for unrealized wishes, present for hopeful wishes). This expression is uniquely Levantine and does not exist in MSA.

examples.english: I wish I was here
examples.breakdown: يا ريت (I wish) + كنت (I was) + هون (here)

examples.english: I wish I could
I wish he'd come back
examples.breakdown: يا ريت (I wish) + بيرجع (he comes back)

examples.english: I wish I hadn't told him
examples.breakdown: يا ريت (I wish) + ما (not) + قلتلو (I told him)

title: Object Pronouns (Attached to Verbs)
explanation: In Lebanese Arabic, object pronouns attach directly to the end of the verb as suffixes. The base verb changes slightly depending on whether it ends in a consonant or vowel. Here are the suffixes shown with شاف (to see).

examples.english: He saw me at the market
I told him about the matter
I gave it to her
I've known them for a long time

title: Connecting Sentences — يعني، هيدا، بس
explanation: Lebanese conversation flows with small connecting words that glue ideas together. These fillers and connectors are essential for sounding natural. They rarely translate directly but serve important discourse functions.

examples.english: I like coffee, but without sugar
This means we can't go
Okay, so what do you want to do?
I also love Lebanese food
